title: Quarter-bound book
subtitle: A case-bound journal with book cloth spine and plain paper sides
excerpt: Quarter binding divides the covering material between a cloth spine and plain or decorative paper sides. The spine cloth carries the strain of opening; the paper sides show pattern or colour. Together they make a book that is stronger than an all-paper binding and more economical than full cloth.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on quarter and half bindings. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. The proportions and mitring method follow Cockerell's instructions.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding style in which the spine is covered with one material (usually cloth or leather) and the boards with a second, lighter material (paper or thin cloth). The cloth spine strip extends one-quarter of the way across each board.
A binding in which the text block is sewn and glued separately from the hard cover case; the two are joined by pasting down the endpapers against the boards.
The amount of covering material folded over the edge of a bookboard to the inside face. A standard turn-in is 15 to 20 mm on each edge.

content.text: Quarter binding has been the practical bookbinder's default for centuries: the
quarter-binding
structure puts strong book cloth exactly where the binding is under stress (the spine and the spine joints) and allows a lighter, more decorative paper to cover the rest. This pattern makes an A5
case-bound
journal with 64 pages of 90 gsm cartridge, a 2 mm board case, and book cloth spine panels.
What you need

attrs.heading: Materials for one A5 quarter-bound journal

items.name: Cartridge paper, 90 gsm, A4
items.qty: 8 sheets (fold each to A5, producing 8 signatures of 4 pages each = 32 leaves = 64 pages)

items.name: Bookbinding board, 2 mm
items.qty: Two pieces cut to A5 height + 3 mm × A5 width + 3 mm

items.name: Book cloth
items.qty: One strip: A5 height + 40 mm × spine thickness + 70 mm

items.name: Decorative or plain paper, 120 gsm
items.qty: Two pieces: A5 height + 40 mm × board width + 20 mm (the side covers)

items.name: Endpaper stock, 120-140 gsm
items.qty: Two A4 sheets (each folds to make front and back endpaper pairs)

content.text: Prepare the text block

Fold each A4 sheet in half to A5 portrait: lay the sheet on the cutting mat with the grain direction running head-to-tail (parallel to the fold line), bone-fold firmly along the centre crease, then nest eight folded sheets together into one signature of 32 pages. Make eight such signatures. Pierce three evenly spaced sewing stations through each signature spine using the bookbinding awl held in the piercing-jig cradle.

Sew the signatures together on three tapes or cord lengths using a simple on-tape sewing pattern: start at the tail kettle stitch, sew through to the head, link each new signature with a kettle stitch. When all eight signatures are sewn, work a final kettle stitch, tie off, and trim the ends.
Glue up and dry the spine

Fan the text block open, brush dilute PVA (2 parts PVA : 1 part water) into the spine with a stiff brush, working the adhesive between the signatures. Let it soak for a minute, then close the text block and press it flat under boards and weights for 30 minutes. Once set, measure the spine thickness; this is the dimension that determines the spine cloth strip width.
Cut the boards and the spine cloth

Cut two bookbinding boards 3 mm taller and 3 mm wider than the text-block leaves. The 3 mm overhang at head, tail, and foredge is the 'square'. Cut the book cloth strip: height equals board height plus two
turn-ins
of 20 mm each; width equals spine thickness plus 35 mm onto each board (one-quarter of the board width, typically). The spine cloth strip will overlap the boards by 35 mm on each side.
Attach the spine cloth

Mark the board position on the reverse of the cloth strip: the gap between the two boards equals the spine thickness plus 6 mm (3 mm joint clearance each side). Brush PVA onto the boards' spine edges and the cloth's gluing area, wait 30 seconds, and set both boards into position. Fold the turn-ins at head and tail over the board edges, mitreing the corners at 45°. Bone-fold along all creases. Let the spine assembly press flat for at least 20 minutes.
Apply the paper sides

Cut two pieces of decorative or plain paper to board height plus 40 mm and board width minus the spine-cloth overlap plus 20 mm. The paper butts up against the spine cloth, overlapping by 3 mm. Brush PVA onto each paper panel, position, smooth from the spine edge outward (never from the middle, which traps air). Fold the turn-ins over the board edges and mitre the corners. Press under a flat board for 30 minutes each side.
Paste in the text block

Fold and glue the endpapers: the inner leaf pastes down against the board, the outer leaf folds into the text block as its first and last leaf. Brush PVA onto the outer face of the endpaper, slip a waste sheet under it to protect the text block, and press the endpaper flat against the board. Close and press overnight.
Finishing and care

Once the paste-down has dried fully (at least 12 hours under weight), the boards should lie flat. If they bow inward (the 'dish' effect from the wet paste-down), this usually self-corrects overnight. If bowing persists, re-press. Store the finished book upright on a shelf with neighbours on both sides; standing in isolation causes the board to twist slowly over years.
Variations

For a half binding, extend the cloth from the spine onto the board corners as well as the spine: corner triangles of the same cloth, cut to match, give the book a formal Victorian look. Marbled paper sides are the traditional choice for quarter and half bindings in the Arts-and-Crafts tradition; past marbling tutorials provide the paper.
